# Instructions for the Universe AI Assistant

This document provides the **creative and stylistic guidelines** for the AI assistant working on this universe. For workflow rules, project structure, and operational modes (Author Mode / Scene Mode), see [README.md](README.md).

Sections marked with brackets are yours to fill in. The sections that matter most are §7 and §8 — tone calibration and red lines are the **hard constraints** that continuity and audit checks enforce against the prose. Everything else bends to the Golden Rule (the story drives the canon); §7–§8 are the only rules the prose itself must obey.

## 1. Tone & Genre

**[Genre].** [Two or three sentences on what kind of story this is and what the reader comes for.]

- **[Tonal principle]** — [e.g., "Warm but not saccharine — real adults with real problems, delivered with wit"]
- **[What serves what]** — [e.g., "Mysteries serve character — the plot matters, but the group dynamics matter more"]
- **[Setting principle]** — [e.g., "The neighborhood is a character — it should feel lived-in and specific"]

## 2. Prose Style

- **[e.g., Show, don't tell.]** [Especially with emotion — let actions and dialogue carry the weight.]
- **[e.g., Sensory-first.]** [Ground scenes in what characters see, smell, hear, taste, and touch.]
- **[e.g., Tight paragraphs.]** [Keep the pace moving even in character moments.]

## 3. Dialogue

- **[e.g., Natural and sharp.]** [Characters interrupt each other. Conversations overlap.]
- **Distinct voices.** Each character should sound different. Keep a voice guide in `world building/` with register details as the cast grows.
- **["Said" 80% of the time]** — [it's invisible. Use character actions instead of colorful tags.]
- **Distinctive tics once per scene, not every line.** A dialect, a verbal habit, a nervous energy — used sparingly it characterizes; used constantly it's a costume.

## 4. Character Voice Quick Reference

_(Expand this table as the cast develops; graduate it to a full `world building/02_character_voice_guide.md` when rows stop fitting.)_

| Character | Public Mode | Under Pressure | Key Contradiction |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|-------------------|
| **[Name]** | [How they present to the world] | [What breaks through under stress] | [The tension that makes them interesting] |
| **[Name]** | | | |

**Key principles:**
- Let contradictions surface gradually across scenes, not all at once
- Silence and deflection are characterization tools
- Characters should speak differently in later stories than in the first — growth should be audible

## 5. POV & Perspective

[Define your POV model and its hard rules. Be precise — this is the section continuity checks quote back at the prose. Questions to answer: Whose perspective anchors the story? When (if ever) may the narrative leave it? What does the reader get to know that characters don't?]

- **No head-hopping within a scene.** Inside any scene, stay in one character's limited perspective — no mind-reading. _(Keep this rule unless your POV model is deliberately omniscient.)_

## 6. Story Construction

[The structural formula for a typical story in this universe, if there is one. E.g., for a mystery: one central mystery + one B-plot, 2–3 red herrings, a maximum clue count, a fair-play rule. For other genres: the beat structure, the recurring set pieces, what every story must and must not contain.]

## 7. Tone Calibration

[A shared numeric scale is the cheapest tool for keeping many scenes consistent — and it makes "this scene is too intense" an objective claim instead of a feeling.]

Most scenes live at **[3–5]** on a 10-point scale. Peaks hit **[6–7]**. Never exceed **[7]**.

| Tone Level | What It Feels Like |
|------------|-------------------|
| 1–2 | [e.g., Light banter, zero tension] |
| 3–5 | **Sweet spot:** [describe the default register of your universe] |
| 6–7 | **Peak:** [real consequences, genuine worry — but still within genre] |
| 8+ | **Never go here.** [Name the genre you refuse to become.] |

## 8. Red Lines

Hard constraints. Prose that crosses one of these is wrong even if the canon library agrees with it.

- [e.g., No on-page violence]
- [e.g., The antagonist stays human — self-interested, not evil]
- [e.g., No deus ex machina resolutions]
- No head-hopping within a scene (see §5)

## 9. Recurring Anchor Element

[Optional but powerful: a recurring element that appears in every story and carries emotional state — a shared meal, a ritual, a journey, a performance. Give it a table mapping story phase → emotional state → how the anchor element reflects it. Anchors give readers a rhythm and give you a barometer for pacing.]

## 10. Formatting

- All output should be in Markdown.
- Use H2 (`##`) for story titles and H3 (`###`) for chapter titles or section headings.
- Dialogue uses standard double quotes (`""`).
